phase.title: Initial System Enumeration
phase.summary: Fingerprint the host: OS version, architecture, patches, drives, network, processes. Everything downstream depends on this.

nodes.title: systeminfo / OS fingerprint
nodes.summary: Identify OS name, version, build number, and architecture. Drives Windows-Exploit-Suggester later.
nodes.description_md: `systeminfo` is the canonical first command on every Windows target. The OS Name + Version + Build + Hotfix list is the input to kernel-exploit suggestion tools like wesng/Watson.

snippets.title: Core OS fields
snippets.code: systeminfo | findstr /B /C:"OS Name" /C:"OS Version" /C:"System Type" /C:"Host Name"

snippets.title: Get-ComputerInfo (PS 5+)
snippets.code: Get-ComputerInfo | Select-Object CsName, OsName, OsVersion, OsBuildNumber, OsArchitecture, OsHotFixes

nodes.detection_hints: `systeminfo.exe` from non-admin user contexts; Sysmon 1 with command line `systeminfo`.

nodes.title: Installed patches (KB list)
nodes.summary: List installed hotfixes; missing KBs map to public kernel exploits.
nodes.description_md: Feed the KB list into wesng or Watson to suggest candidate CVEs.

nodes.title: Process architecture (x86 / x64)
nodes.summary: Choose the right payload + tool architecture for the host.
nodes.description_md: Some Potato variants and kernel exploits are arch-specific. KiTrap0D is x86-only; JuicyPotato is x64-only.

nodes.title: Environment variables
nodes.summary: Read PATH, TEMP, USERPROFILE, and any custom vars that may contain credentials or hint at app installs.
nodes.description_md: Custom env vars set by sysadmins occasionally contain plaintext passwords or API tokens.

nodes.title: Drives & mounted shares
nodes.summary: Enumerate logical drives, mapped network shares, and removable media that may contain credentials or backups.
nodes.description_md: Watch for non-default drives (D:, E:) — they often host backups, code repositories, or legacy installations with weak ACLs.

nodes.title: Network interfaces, routes & listeners
nodes.summary: Identify dual-homed hosts, internal subnets, and locally bound services that may be exploitable.
nodes.description_md: Bound-to-localhost services often skip authentication and are excellent privesc surfaces.

nodes.title: Installed software
nodes.summary: Third-party software is the #1 source of writable service binaries and unquoted paths.
nodes.description_md: `wmic product` is reliable but slow (it triggers MSI repair). Prefer the registry uninstall keys for speed.

snippets.title: Registry uninstall keys (fast)
snippets.code:
Get-ItemProperty HKLM:\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Uninstall\*, HKLM:\Software\Wow6432Node\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Uninstall\* |
  Select-Object DisplayName, DisplayVersion, Publisher, InstallLocation |
  Where-Object DisplayName

nodes.title: Running processes & owners
nodes.summary: Find services running as SYSTEM with binaries in non-standard paths — prime hijack targets.
nodes.description_md: Cross-reference process image paths with writable directories from later phases (`path-*`, `svc-*`).

snippets.title: Process + owner
snippets.code: Get-CimInstance Win32_Process | Select-Object Name, ProcessId, ExecutablePath, @{N='Owner';E={ ($_ | Invoke-CimMethod -MethodName GetOwner).User }}

nodes.title: Pending reboot / staged updates
nodes.summary: Hosts with staged updates may be missing a fix that *would* close your foothold — exploit before the next reboot.
nodes.description_md: Several registry signals indicate a pending reboot.

nodes.title: OS build & version detail
nodes.summary: Pin the exact Windows build so kernel-exploit and bypass selection is precise.
nodes.description_md: `systeminfo` is broad; the **build number** is what drives exploit choice. Read it from the registry or `ver`. Map the build to a release (e.g. 19045 = 22H2) before picking a Potato variant or kernel CVE.

snippets.title: Build from registry
snippets.code: Get-ItemProperty 'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion' | Select-Object ProductName, DisplayVersion, CurrentBuild, UBR

nodes.title: Installed patches via WMIC QFE
nodes.summary: List applied hotfixes to find missing patches a kernel exploit can target.
nodes.description_md: `wmic qfe` enumerates Quick-Fix Engineering updates. Cross-reference the list against known privesc CVEs (or feed it to **wesng** / Watson). A short list of recent KBs often means an unpatched box.

nodes.title: Windows Firewall state
nodes.summary: Inspect firewall profiles & rules to plan reverse shells and pivoting.
nodes.description_md: Knowing which outbound ports are allowed shapes your callback choice. Disabled profiles or permissive outbound rules mean a reverse shell on 443/53 is likely to succeed.

snippets.title: Dump rules
snippets.code: netsh advfirewall firewall show rule name=all

nodes.title: Microsoft Defender status
nodes.summary: Check real-time protection, tamper protection, and exclusion paths.
nodes.description_md: `Get-MpComputerStatus` reveals whether real-time protection is on. `Get-MpPreference` lists **exclusion paths/extensions** — dropping your tooling into an excluded folder sidesteps AV entirely.

nodes.title: Installed AV / EDR products
nodes.summary: Identify the endpoint security product to tune evasion.
nodes.description_md: The `AntiVirusProduct` WMI namespace lists registered security products. Process and service names also fingerprint EDRs (e.g. `cb.exe`, `SentinelAgent`, `csfalcon`).

nodes.title: AppLocker / WDAC policy
nodes.summary: Read the application-control policy to find allowed execution paths.
nodes.description_md: AppLocker rules frequently allow execution from writable paths (e.g. `C:\Windows\Tasks`, `C:\Windows\Temp`). Enumerate the effective policy to find an allowed directory you can write to.

nodes.title: LAPS presence
nodes.summary: Detect LAPS so you know local-admin passwords may be AD-readable.
nodes.description_md: If `AdmPwd.dll` or the LAPS CSE is present, the local administrator password is randomized and stored in AD (`ms-Mcs-AdmPwd`). Readable by delegated principals — note it for the AD bridge.

snippets.title: Check for LAPS CSE
snippets.code: Get-ChildItem 'C:\Program Files\LAPS\CSE\AdmPwd.dll' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue; Get-ItemProperty 'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft Services\AdmPwd' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue

nodes.title: Integrity level & UAC config
nodes.summary: Determine your integrity level and whether UAC bypass is needed.
nodes.description_md: If you're a member of Administrators but running at **Medium** integrity, you need a UAC bypass to get a High-integrity token. `whoami /groups` shows the integrity label; the registry shows the UAC posture (`EnableLUA`, `ConsentPromptBehaviorAdmin`).

snippets.title: UAC posture
snippets.code: reg query HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System /v EnableLUA & reg query HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System /v ConsentPromptBehaviorAdmin

nodes.title: Mapped & local shares
nodes.summary: List SMB shares and mapped drives that may expose credentials or write targets.
nodes.description_md: Network shares often hold scripts, backups, and config files with credentials. Writable shares referenced by SYSTEM services or scheduled tasks are direct privesc vectors.

snippets.title: Local shares & mappings
snippets.code: net share & net use & wmic share get name,path

nodes.title: PowerShell version & language mode
nodes.summary: Check PS version and whether Constrained Language Mode is enforced.
nodes.description_md: `$ExecutionContext.SessionState.LanguageMode` returns `FullLanguage` or `ConstrainedLanguage`. CLM blocks many offensive cmdlets — note it so you can plan a CLM bypass. PS v2 (if present) sidesteps AMSI and ScriptBlock logging.

nodes.title: Scheduled tasks overview
nodes.summary: Quick inventory of non-Microsoft scheduled tasks to triage in phase 8.
nodes.description_md: A fast first pass: list tasks and flag any running as SYSTEM or pointing at non-standard paths. Deep analysis (writable scripts/binaries/ACLs) lives in the Scheduled Tasks phase.

snippets.title: List tasks
snippets.code: schtasks /query /fo LIST /v | findstr /i "TaskName Run As User Task To Run"

nodes.title: Named pipe enumeration
nodes.summary: List named pipes to spot impersonation and service IPC opportunities.
nodes.description_md: Named pipes exposed by privileged services can enable impersonation primitives. Enumerate them and note any with weak ACLs or owned by SYSTEM services.
